tldr: Headlines across all 17 buckets frame this as a suicide bomb attack on a train in Pakistan's Balochistan province, with death tolls ranging from 14 to 47 depending on outlet and update timing. Two outlets — Turkey's Anadolu Agency and Iran's IRNA — chose diplomatic-condemnation angles over the attack itself, while India's Republic World was the only outlet to name the Balochistan Liberation Army and invoke the term 'Fidayeen' in its headline.

frames.sub_frame: Suicide bomb attack on military train

evidence.quote: More than a dozen killed as bomb explodes near train
Blast targeting train kills at least 20 in Pakistan
Suicide bombing near a railway track in southwest Pakistan
Blast targets military train in Pakistan, 24 dead
China condemns deadly Pakistan train attack
Iran condemns terrorist attack on Pakistan's Quetta railway

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to name the BLA by name and use the Islamist-militant framing term 'Fidayeen' — presenting the attack through religious-ideological identity rather than the separatist-insurgency framing used by all other outlets.

Leads with vivid physical-damage imagery ('Train windows blown out, cars mangled') and notes soldiers were on Eid leave — the only headline to humanise military victims through a cultural/holiday context rather than a bare casualty count.

bottom_line: Headline coverage universally frames the Quetta train bombing as a security/terrorism event with military victims; the only structural divergences are diplomatic-condemnation leads from Turkish and Iranian state outlets, and India's Republic World uniquely invoking BLA and 'Fidayeen' framing while wire services and most international outlets stayed with factual attack-and-casualty language.
